name: Direct and Inverse Proportion
whatIsIt: In DIRECT proportion, two quantities grow (or shrink) together at the same rate — more items, more cost; double one, double the other. In INVERSE proportion, one goes UP while the other goes DOWN — more workers, less time; double the workers, halve the time. Spotting which kind you have tells you whether to multiply or divide.
whyNeeded: Recipes, shopping, travel, and work planning all rely on proportion — scaling a recipe up, working out cost for more items, or how long a job takes with more people. It's the practical heart of ratios and a big idea in science.
realLifeUses: 3 workers finish in 6 days, so 6 workers finish in… (inverse)
Doubling a recipe doubles every ingredient (direct)
Faster speed means less time for the same distance (inverse)

vocabulary.meaning: Both quantities increase or decrease together at the same rate.
As one increases, the other decreases (their product stays fixed).
Find the value of ONE first, then scale to the amount you need.

story.title: The Wall Painters
story.text: Ravi sees 5 pens cost ₹50 and needs 8. He finds one pen: 50 ÷ 5 = ₹10, so 8 cost ₹80 — direct proportion. Then the foreman says 3 painters finish a wall in 6 days; with 6 painters? Ravi thinks: twice the painters, half the time — 3 days. 'That's inverse,' he says, 'more workers, less time.'
story.extractedProblem: 5 pens cost ₹50. What do 8 pens cost?

visual.caption: One pen = ₹10. Direct proportion: more pens → proportionally more cost (8 × 10 = ₹80).

teachingGallery.title: Together (direct) or opposite (inverse)
teachingGallery.note: Direct: ×/÷ the same way. Inverse: one up, the other down.

standardMethod.summary: Decide direct or inverse, then use the unitary method (direct: find one, multiply) or keep the product fixed (inverse).
steps: Ask: do both grow together (direct) or does one grow as the other shrinks (inverse)?
DIRECT: find the value of ONE (divide), then multiply for the amount you need.
INVERSE: the product stays the same — total = quantity × the other quantity; solve for the missing one.
Check the answer makes sense: more should cost more (direct); more workers should take less time (inverse).

workedExamples.problem: If 4 kg of rice cost ₹200, what do 7 kg cost?
6 taps fill a tank in 4 hours. How long for 8 taps?

commonMistakes.mistake: Multiplying up for an inverse situation (more workers → more time).
commonMistakes.fix: In inverse proportion, more of one means LESS of the other. Keep their PRODUCT fixed.

commonMistakes.mistake: Scaling without first finding the value of one unit.
commonMistakes.fix: Use the unitary method: find the cost/time for ONE first, then scale to the number you need.

easy.q: 1 pen costs ₹10. What do 6 pens cost (in ₹)?
More workers to do a job means the time is…

medium.q: 3 kg cost ₹90. What do 5 kg cost (in ₹)?
4 workers finish in 6 days. How many days for 8 workers?

challenge.q: 5 books cost ₹250. What do 9 books cost (in ₹)?
6 taps fill a tank in 2 hours. Ravi says 12 taps take 4 hours. What's the fix?

questions.q: 1 kg costs ₹40. What do 5 kg cost (in ₹)?
2 kg cost ₹100. What does 1 kg cost (in ₹)?
3 workers finish in 8 days. How many days for 6 workers?
When two quantities grow together at the same rate, it is…

teachBackPrompt: Give a grown-up two everyday examples: one where more means more (buying more fruit costs more — direct), and one where more means less (more people share a chore, so it's faster — inverse). Explain how you'd calculate each.

revisionCard.summary: Direct proportion: both change together — find ONE (divide), then multiply. Inverse proportion: one up, the other down — keep the PRODUCT fixed (quantity × other = constant). Always sanity-check: more should cost more (direct), more workers → less time (inverse).

rememberIt.hook: Proportion = two equal ratios. Cross-multiply to solve
rememberIt.unpack: a/b = c/d means ad = bc. Direct: both grow together. Inverse: one up, other down (speed vs time).

studentQuestions.q: How do I solve a/b = c/x?
studentQuestions.a: Cross-multiply: a x = b c, then divide. It finds the value that keeps the ratio equal.
Direct: more of one, more of the other (more items, more cost). Inverse: faster speed, less time.
